---
title: "Decimals, significant figures, and plots"
output:
  beamer_presentation:
    theme: "default"
    colortheme: "default"
    fonttheme: "default"
  ioslides_presentation: default
  slidy_presentation: default
header-includes:
- \usepackage{enumerate}
- \usepackage{hyperref}
- \usepackage{caption}
- \usepackage[normalem]{ulem}
- \definecolor{links}{HTML}{2A1B81}
- \hypersetup{colorlinks,linkcolor=,urlcolor=links}
colorlinks: true
linkcolor: blue
urlcolor: blue
---

```{r, echo = FALSE}
library(knitr);
opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE);
```


## Decimal places

\LARGE

Digits after the decimal point (including 0s)

Value: **1.23041**

\begin{itemize}
\item 5 Decimals: 1.23041
\item 4 Decimals: 1.2304
\item 3 Decimals: 1.230
\item 2 Decimals: 1.23
\item 1 Decimals: 1.2
\end{itemize}


## Decimal places

\LARGE

Digits after the decimal point (including 0s)

Value: **1430.02021**

\begin{itemize}
\item 5 Decimals: 1430.02021
\item 4 Decimals: 1430.0202
\item 3 Decimals: 1430.020
\item 2 Decimals: 1430.02
\item 1 Decimals: 1430.0
\end{itemize}


## Significant figures

\Huge

- **All** digits of a number
- Initial 0s don't count
- Other 0s do count

\vspace{-3mm}
\begin{center}
\rule{\textwidth}{1mm}
\end{center}
\vspace{-1mm}

- 21.341
- 0.032
- 0.010230

## Significant figures

\Huge

Digits needed to infer the accuracy of a value$^{1}$

- 240.23432 implies more accuracy than 240
- 1.324 implies more accuracy than 1.3

\footnotetext[1]{Rahman, N A. 1968. A Course in Theoretical Statistics. Charles Griffin \& Company, London.}


## Significant figures

\Huge

- **All** digits of a number
- Initial 0s don't count
- Other 0s do count

\vspace{-3mm}
\begin{center}
\rule{\textwidth}{1mm}
\end{center}
\vspace{-1mm}

- 0.32400
- 0.00031
- 2000



## Rounding to specific digits or significant figures

\LARGE

**Rules for rounding:**

- Round based on digit to the right
- If digit is [0, 1, 2, 3, 4] round down
- If digit is [5, 6, 7, 8, 9] round up
- Round down keeps digit the same
- Rounding up increases digit by 1

\LARGE
\vspace{2mm}

**Round 0.1234 to 3 decimal places:**

$$0.1234 \to 0.123$$











## Rounding to specific digits or significant figures

\LARGE

**Rules for rounding:**

- Round based on digit to the right
- If digit is [0, 1, 2, 3, 4] round down
- If digit is [5, 6, 7, 8, 9] round up
- Round down keeps digit the same
- Rounding up increases digit by 1

\LARGE
\vspace{2mm}

**Round 0.1235 to 3 decimal places:**

$$0.1235 \to 0.124$$



## Rounding to specific digits or significant figures

\LARGE

**Rules for rounding:**

- Round based on digit to the right
- If digit is [0, 1, 2, 3, 4] round down
- If digit is [5, 6, 7, 8, 9] round up
- Round down keeps digit the same
- Rounding up increases digit by 1

\LARGE
\vspace{2mm}

**Round 3.184 to 3 significant figures:**

$$3.184 \to 3.18$$


## Rounding to specific digits or significant figures

\LARGE

**Rules for rounding:**

- Round based on digit to the right
- If digit is [0, 1, 2, 3, 4] round down
- If digit is [5, 6, 7, 8, 9] round up
- Round down keeps digit the same
- Rounding up increases digit by 1

\LARGE
\vspace{2mm}

**Round 3.185 to 3 significant figures:**

$$3.185 \to 3.19$$



## Rounding to specific digits or significant figures

\LARGE

**Rules for rounding:**

- Round based on digit to the right
- If digit is [0, 1, 2, 3, 4] round down
- If digit is [5, 6, 7, 8, 9] round up
- Round down keeps digit the same
- Rounding up increases digit by 1

\LARGE
\vspace{2mm}

**Round 3.195 to 3 significant figures:**

$$3.195 \to 3.20$$



## Rounding to specific digits or significant figures

\LARGE

**Rules for rounding:**

- Round based on digit to the right
- If digit is [0, 1, 2, 3, 4] round down
- If digit is [5, 6, 7, 8, 9] round up
- Round down keeps digit the same
- Rounding up increases digit by 1

\LARGE
\vspace{2mm}

**Round 12.9806 to 3 significant figures:**

$$12.9806 \to 13.0$$


## Rounding to specific digits or significant figures

\LARGE

**Rules for rounding:**

- Round based on digit to the right
- If digit is [0, 1, 2, 3, 4] round down
- If digit is [5, 6, 7, 8, 9] round up
- Round down keeps digit the same
- Rounding up increases digit by 1

\LARGE
\vspace{2mm}

**Round 12.9806 to 1 significant figure:**

$$12.9806 \to 10$$


## Rounding to specific digits or significant figures

\LARGE

**Rules for rounding:**

- Round based on digit to the right
- If digit is [0, 1, 2, 3, 4] round down
- If digit is [5, 6, 7, 8, 9] round up
- Round down keeps digit the same
- Rounding up increases digit by 1

\LARGE
\vspace{2mm}

**Round 10.023 to 2 significant figures:**

$$10.023 \to 10.$$


## Can indicate significant figures with scientific notation

\Huge

**2000 in significant figures:**

\vspace{4mm}

\begin{enumerate}
\item $2 \times 10^{3}$ \vspace{-1mm}
\item $2.0 \times 10^{3}$  \vspace{-1mm}
\item $2.00 \times 10^{3}$  \vspace{-1mm}
\item $2.000 \times 10^{3}$  \vspace{-1mm}
\end{enumerate}


## Data visualisation

\Huge

- Histograms
- Barplots
- Pie Charts
- Box-whisker plots


## Histogram

\Huge

- Important visualisation tool
- Show **distribution** of *continuous* data
- Need for statistical decision-making
- How to [build a histogram](https://bradduthie.github.io/stats/app/build_histogram/)


## Histogram


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "An example histogram is shown with a somewhat normal shape", fig.cap = "Fig fruit width (cm) using data from 78 fig fruits.", out.width="100%"}
fig_fruits  <- read.csv(file = "data/fig_fruits.csv");
par(mar = c(5, 5, 2, 1));
hist(x = fig_fruits[["Width_cm"]], xlab = "Fig fruit width (cm)", 
     ylab = "Frequency", cex.lab = 2, col = "blue", main = "", cex.axis = 2,
     breaks = 5, xlim = c(0.8, 2.2));
```


## Histogram


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "An example histogram is shown with a somewhat normal shape", fig.cap = "Fig fruit width (cm) using data from 78 fig fruits.", out.width="100%"}
par(mar = c(5, 5, 2, 1));
new_breaks <- seq(from = 0.8, to = 2.2, by = 0.1);
hist(x = fig_fruits[["Width_cm"]], xlab = "Fig fruit width (cm)", 
     ylab = "Frequency", cex.lab = 2, col = "blue", main = "", cex.axis = 2,
     breaks = new_breaks, xlim = c(0.8, 2.2));
```



## Histogram


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "An example histogram is shown with a somewhat normal shape", fig.cap = "Fig fruit width (cm) using data from 78 fig fruits.", out.width="100%"}
new_breaks <- c(0.8, 0.9, 1, 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4, 1.5, 1.6, 2.2);
par(mar = c(5, 5, 2, 1));
hist(x = fig_fruits[["Width_cm"]], xlab = "Fig fruit width (cm)", 
     ylab = "Frequency", cex.lab = 2, col = "blue", main = "", cex.axis = 2,
     breaks = new_breaks, xlim = c(0.8, 2.2));
```


## Barplots {.t}

\Huge

- Visualise *categorical* data
- Categories separated in bars
- Heights indicate magnitudes
- Different from histograms

## Barplots


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "An example barplot shows the total number of fruits collected from four trees labelled A, B, C, and D.", fig.cap = "Fruits from each of four trees (78 total).", out.width="100%"}
fruits_per_tree <- table(fig_fruits[["Tree"]]);
par(mar = c(5, 5, 2, 1));
barplot(fruits_per_tree, xlab = "Tree", ylab = "Fruits sampled", cex.lab = 2, 
        cex.axis = 2, col = "blue", cex.names = 3);
```



## Pie charts {.t}

\Huge

- Visualise *categorical* data
- Show percentage of categories
- Used less often than barplots
- Do not show absolute magnitude


## Pie charts 

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "An example pie chart shows the percentage of fruits collected from four trees labelled A, B, C, and D.", fig.cap = "Pie chart of fruits from 4 trees.", out.width="80%"}
cbPalett <- c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#F0E442", "#0072B2", 
                "#D55E00", "#CC79A7", "#cc0000", "#000066", "#FF6666", 
                "#666633", "#b3b3b3");
pie_fruits_labels <- c("Tree A", "Tree B", "Tree C", "Tree D");
pie(x = fruits_per_tree, labels = pie_fruits_labels,
    col = cbPalett[1:4], main = "");
```


## Barplot versus pie charts


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "An example pie chart shows the percentage of fruits collected from four trees labelled A, B, C, and D.", fig.cap = "Barplot and pie chart of fruits from 4 trees.", out.width="100%", out.height="80%"}
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(5, 5, 1, 1));
barplot(fruits_per_tree, xlab = "Tree", ylab = "Fruits sampled", cex.lab = 2, 
        cex.axis = 2, col = cbPalett[1:4], cex.names = 3);
pie_fruits_labels <- c("Tree A", "Tree B", "Tree C", "Tree D");
pie(x = fruits_per_tree, labels = pie_fruits_labels,
    col = cbPalett[1:4], main = "");
```


## Box-whisker plots (boxplots)

\Huge

- Show distributions
- Visualise summary statistics
- Can compare distributions
- More compact than a histogram (but a bit less information)


## Box-whisker plots (boxplots)

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Example of boxplot showing the distributions of fig fruit width for four different trees.", fig.cap = "Boxplot of fig fruit widths (cm) collected from 4 trees.", out.width="100%"}
par(mar = c(5, 5, 1, 1));
boxplot(formula = fig_fruits[["Width_cm"]]~fig_fruits[["Tree"]], ylab = "Fig fruit width (cm)", xlab = "Tree", cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2, col = "blue", lwd = 3);
```

## Boxplot versus histogram

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Example of a simple boxplot shown to the left of a histogram, each showing the same information, with points showing how properties of the two panels match.", fig.cap = "Boxplot (A) vs Histogram (B) of fig fruit widths (cm)", out.width="100%"}
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(5, 5, 1, 1));
boxplot(x = fig_fruits[["Width_cm"]], ylab = "Fig fruit width (cm)", xlab = "All trees", cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2, col = cbPalett[2]);
text(x = 0.6, y = 2.066, labels = "A", cex = 2.5);
sum_fig_fruits <- summary(fig_fruits[["Width_cm"]]);
points(y = as.numeric(sum_fig_fruits[3]), x = 1, pch = 16, cex = 5, 
       col = cbPalett[1]);
points(y = as.numeric(sum_fig_fruits[2]), x = 1, pch = 15, cex = 4.5, 
       col = cbPalett[3]);
points(y = as.numeric(sum_fig_fruits[5]), x = 1, pch = 15, cex = 4.5, 
       col = cbPalett[3]);
points(y = as.numeric(sum_fig_fruits[1]), x = 1, pch = 17, cex = 4.5, 
       col = cbPalett[4]);
points(y = as.numeric(sum_fig_fruits[6]), x = 1, pch = 17, cex = 4.5, 
       col = cbPalett[4]);
hist(x = fig_fruits[["Width_cm"]], xlab = "Fig fruit width (cm)",
     ylab = "Frequency", cex.lab = 2, col = cbPalett[2], main = "", 
     breaks = 5, xlim = c(0.8, 2.2));
sum_fig_fruits <- summary(fig_fruits[["Width_cm"]]);
points(x = as.numeric(sum_fig_fruits[3]), y = 0, pch = 16, cex = 5, 
       col = cbPalett[1]);
points(x = as.numeric(sum_fig_fruits[2]), y = 0, pch = 15, cex = 4.5, 
       col = cbPalett[3]);
points(x = as.numeric(sum_fig_fruits[5]), y = 0, pch = 15, cex = 4.5, 
       col = cbPalett[3]);
points(x = as.numeric(sum_fig_fruits[1]), y = 0, pch = 17, cex = 4.5, 
       col = cbPalett[4]);
points(x = as.numeric(sum_fig_fruits[6]), y = 0, pch = 17, cex = 4.5, 
       col = cbPalett[4]);
text(x = 0.9, y = 27, labels = "B", cex = 2.5);
par(mfrow = c(1, 1));
```


## Data visualisation principles

\Huge

1. Show the data
2. Make the meaning clear
3. Avoid distorting what the data have to say
4. Make the data accessible
5. Focus on the content

\footnotetext[1]{David Sterratt. 2025. CC BY-SA 4.0 Source: https://github.com/Inf2-FDS/fds-visualisation}


## Data visualisation principles: Show the data

\Huge

- Show as much as possible
- Try to avoid a confusion
- No one "right" visualisation
- Help reader compare several pieces of data


## Data visualisation principles: Show the data

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Complex figure relating inbreeding adjustment, number of mates, and polyandry to the cost mating.", fig.cap = "", out.width="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/Nmin1_mn.png")
```


## Data visualisation principles: Show the data

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Box plots shown on sparrow data with a box for alive and dead.", fig.cap = "Sparrow length (mm) for different survival outcomes following an unusually large storm at Brown University in 1898 (N = 136).", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/sparrows.png")
```


## Make the meaning clear

\Huge

- Informative captions
- Meaningful axis labels
- Relevant units
- Describe error bars


## Avoid distorting what the data have to say

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Two bad pie charts that distort the data, one in 2D and the same information in 3D", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/badpie.png")
```


\LARGE 
\vspace{3mm}

- Need to clearly see patterns in data
- Avoid the use of 3D if possible
- Easier to compare lengths than areas$^{1}$
- Easier to compare lengths than angles$^{1}$

\footnotetext[1]{David Sterratt. 2025. CC BY-SA 4.0 Source: https://github.com/Inf2-FDS/fds-visualisation}
\footnotetext[2]{\textbf{Image:} \href{https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Misleading_Pie_Chart.png}{Public domain}.}


## Avoid distorting what the data have to say

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Two bar charts showing the same data but with different y-axes, with the smaller axis making the comparison look more extreme", fig.cap = "", out.width="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/badbar.png")
```


## Make the data accessible

\LARGE

- Ensure text is legible
- Avoid unncessary colours
- Select colours carefully$^{1}$

\vspace{2mm}

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "An example pie chart shows the percentage of fruits collected from four trees labelled A, B, C, and D.", fig.cap = "", out.width="50%", out.height="50%"}
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(5, 5, 1, 1));
barplot(fruits_per_tree, xlab = "Tree", ylab = "Fruits sampled", cex.lab = 2, 
        cex.axis = 2, col = cbPalett[1:4], cex.names = 3);
pie_fruits_labels <- c("Tree A", "Tree B", "Tree C", "Tree D");
pie(x = fruits_per_tree, labels = pie_fruits_labels,
    col = cbPalett[1:4], main = "");
```

\footnotetext[1]{David Sterratt. 2025. CC BY-SA 4.0 Source: https://github.com/Inf2-FDS/fds-visualisation}
\footnotetext[2]{Wexler, S, et al. 2017. The Big Book of Dashboards: Visualizing Your Data Using Real-World Business Scenarios. Wiley.}


## Focus on the content

\LARGE

- Avoid colours or 3D with no meaning$^{1}$
- Avoid vertical grid lines
- Use consistent colours across a report
- Use an appropriate number of decimals
- Check wording and spelling
- Consider plotting modules in jamovi

\footnotetext[1]{David Sterratt. 2025. CC BY-SA 4.0 Source: https://github.com/Inf2-FDS/fds-visualisation}
